title: Relocated McGrath bears return home
summary: FAIRBANKS -- Several bears moved as part of a predator control experiment near McGrath a little more than a month ago have returned home, and at least four are within sniffing distance, a state biologist said. "They're making their way back," said biologist Mark Keech, with the Alaska Department of Fish and Game in Fairbanks. The state spent $60,000 to capture and relocate 90 bears from a 520-square-mile area surrounding McGrath during a three-week period in May in an attempt to increase the survival of newborn moose calves. Studies have shown that bears kill more newborn moose calves than do wolves. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Security office works out the kinks COSTS: Safeguarding assets with limited funds challenges new agency. By DAN JOLING The Associated Press (Published: June 8, 2003) The head of Alaska's new Office of Homeland Security says one big question remains when it comes to protecting the state's infrastructure against terrorists: Who's going to pay? title: The time is ripe to go after the ravenous fish with fangs
summary: Alexander Lake -- We caught them all day long, from 9 a.m. Sunday when the plane dropped us off until pickup time at 3:30 that afternoon. The fish struck like sharks, charging through sedges -- and sometimes leaping out of the water -- to hammer bass poppers, deer-hair mice and red-and-yellow rabbit-hide streamers. The fish were northern pike, Alexander Lake's species du jour ever since these voracious predators, illegally stocked in the Susitna Valley roughly 50 years ago, arrived here and gobbled up native stocks of rainbow trout and grayling.
